title: 3.3 Eurasian Belief Systems
description: This exercise will cover a discussion of the development of belief systems in land-based empires from 1450 to 1750 CE. Items will focus on the skill of change and continuity in those belief systems and the theme of CDI while covering a diverse range of geographic examples. These geographic examples are: The Sunni/Shi’a rifts in the Middle East, the Protestant Reformation in Europe, and Sikhism in

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best shows the impact of the Protestant Reformation on European society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A picture of an old document written in Latin.
options.caption: Source: First page of the Basel (Switzerland) pamphlet edition of the Ninety-Five Theses (1517 CE) written by Martin Luther in Wittenberg, Germany. Public domain.

choices.content: It disrupted traditional hierarchies.
It maintained the same hierarchies as the Catholic Church.
It taught spiritual equality but practiced a hierarchical society.
It was repressed by rulers in order to maintain a connection to the Catholic Church.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following developments provides context for the pamphlet above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A picture of an old document written in Latin.
options.caption: Source: First page of the Basel (Switzerland) pamphlet edition of the Ninety-Five Theses (1517 CE) written by Martin Luther in Wittenberg, Germany. Public domain.

choices.content: the invention of the printing press
the formation of the Church of England
the Peasant War of 1524
the printing of the first Bible in German

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the best comparison of Protestant beliefs in Europe and Sikh beliefs in South Asia in the early 1500s CE?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both believed that the divine was accessible to all people regardless of social status.
Both sought political power by breaking away from traditional religions.
Protestants emphasized grace, while Sikhs emphasized the caste system.
Protestants sought God in everyday life, while Sikhs sought nirvana through scripture.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which of the following ways did the Protestant Reformation mark a break with existing Roman Catholic tradition?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Protestants believed individuals could have their own relationship with God separate from the Church’s hierarchy.
Protestants believed that in order to have a relationship with God, believers had to go through an elaborate ecclesiastical process to be accepted.
Protestants believed that in order to be absolved of one’s sins, one has to pay “indulgences” to the Church.
Protestants believed that violence in the name of God should be ended, and those who were Protestant became pacifists.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following developments best contextualizes the battle depicted above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Colorful print that shows a man on horseback stabbing another man with a sword.
options.caption: Source: Safavid leader Shah Ismail kills an Ottoman warrior during the battle of Chaldiran (1514 CE). Painted in Isfahan, Iran, c.1650 CE. Public domain.

choices.content: ideological and political differences between Shi’a and Sunni sects in Islam
the growth of lucrative trade routes through both empires
conflict over beliefs of the mystical Sufi community and the Shi’a Twelver sect in Islam
growing discontent with the absolutist rulers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best illustrates the author’s purpose for painting the image above?  Choose the best answer.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Colorful print that shows a man on horseback stabbing another man with a sword.
options.caption: Source: Safavid leader Shah Ismail kills an Ottoman warrior during the battle of Chaldiran (1514 CE). Painted in Isfahan, Iran, c.1650 CE. Public domain.

choices.content: dramatizing the heroism of a founding ruler
informing later generations of the details of an important recent event
accurately depicting the rise of an emperor
outlining the challenges of building an empire

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a continuity in the belief systems of the Ottoman and Safavid Empires between 1450 and 1750?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: While the political fighting between the empires intensified the rift between Sunni and Shi’a Islam, the divide existed before and after the empires rose and fell.
While the political fighting between the empires created a rift between Sunni and Shi’a Islam, the religion remained largely united around those who succeeded Muhammad.
While the Ottoman and Safavid Empires struggled over some territorial issues, the two empires were largely united against the Mughal threat to their south.
While the Ottoman and the Safavid Empires had differing ideas about Sufism, their respective issues with Christianity kept the empires united.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the rise of the Safavid Empire represent a change in Islamic belief systems from 1450 to 1750?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Safavid Empire followed Shi’a Islam, intensifying the rift between Sunni and Shi’a Muslims.
The Safavid Empire followed Sunni Islam, which united all the Muslim empires under one overarching belief system.
The Safavid Empire followed Shi’a Islam but did not use religion to consolidate its power, creating a secular empire.
The Safavid Empire followed Sunni Islam, which gave rise to a new form of Islam called Sufism.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following developments do the major religious changes that occurred in Eurasia between 1450 and 1750 all have in common?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: urbanization, the rise of absolutism, inflation in the economy, and societal shifts
the emergence of taxation intensified war between nations, and increased social equality
new agricultural methods, the growth of trade networks, and political unification under major empires
climate change, the rise of monarchies, economic growth, and improved rural life

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>My father always associated with the learned of every creed and religion, especially with Pandits and the learned of India ...


>[During my reign] at the city of [Varanasi] a temple had been erected ... The principal idol in this temple had on its head a tiara ... enriched with jewels ... It was the belief of these [non-Muslims] that a dead Hindu ... when laid before this idol would be restored to life. As I could not possibly give credit to such a pretense, I employed a confidential person to ascertain the truth; and, as I justly supposed, the whole was detected to be [a deception] ...[so I destroyed] this temple ... and on the spot, with the very same materials, I erected the great mosque, because the very name of Islam was proscribed at [Varanasi], and with God’s blessing it is my design, if I live, to fill it with true believers.

Source: The Memoirs of Jahangir, Mughal Emperor Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir, 1625. OER


**Which of the following changes within belief systems during the Mughal Empire’s rule over India does the excerpt above best represent?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Later emperors in the Mughal Empire extended less tolerance to non-Muslim subjects.
Later emperors in the Mughal Empire extended more tolerance to non-Muslim subjects.
Mughal rulers blended Hindu traditions into Muslim practices throughout the course of the empire.
Mughal rulers made an effort to create spaces for Muslims to worship safely.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

>My father always associated with the learned of every creed and religion, especially with Pandits and the learned of India ...


>[During my reign] at the city of [Varanasi] a temple had been erected ... The principal idol in this temple had on its head a tiara ... enriched with jewels ... It was the belief of these [non-Muslims] that a dead Hindu ... when laid before this idol would be restored to life. As I could not possibly give credit to such a pretense, I employed a confidential person to ascertain the truth; and, as I justly supposed, the whole was detected to be [a deception] ...[so I destroyed] this temple ... and on the spot, with the very same materials, I erected the great mosque, because the very name of Islam was proscribed at [Varanasi], and with God’s blessing it is my design, if I live, to fill it with true believers.

Source: The Memoirs of Jahangir, Mughal Emperor Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir, 1625. OER

**Which of the following best provides historical context for the passage above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: In the early Mughal Empire, Islamic and Hindu communities co-existed.
In the late Mughal Empire, Hinduism dominated Islam.
The rise of Sikhism led to the widespread persecution of both Hindus and Muslims.
Many leaders made an attempt to create a more secular society that accepted those of Islamic, Hindu, and Sikh traditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes the social impact of the rise of Sikhism in the Mughal Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It threatened existing caste divisions.
It caused people to follow multiple religions at once.
It made Mughal emperors more tolerant.
It lowered the status of Muslims.
